message:
feat(core,journal,btrfs,dir): add fast-commit evidence collection, chunk tree walking, and recovery hardening

Fast-commit evidence (ffs-core):
- Add mount-time fast-commit evidence collection (non-blocking,
  diagnostic) for ext4 journal segment resolution and extent trimming

Btrfs (ffs-btrfs):
- Extend chunk/device tree walking infrastructure

Directory (ffs-dir):
- Fix directory entry operations and coalescing logic

Journal (ffs-journal):
- Update fast-commit corpus tests and replay logic
- Update Cargo.toml dependencies

Documentation and tracking updates.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-h6nz","title":"EPIC: V1.1 Post-Parity Hardening and Operationalization","description":"# EPIC: V1.1 Post-Parity Hardening and Operationalization

## Context
Tracked V1 parity is complete (75/75), but FrankenFS is explicitly still early-stage and experimental for mount/write operations. The project now needs a rigorous post-parity program focused on production-grade correctness confidence, failure containment, observability, and predictable performance under stress.

This epic converts that post-parity intent into an executable dependency graph that is:
- granular enough for independent agent execution,
- explicit about acceptance gates,
- self-contained (no need to reopen planning docs during execution).

## Why this exists
Parity completion proves breadth of implemented behavior, not necessarily depth of operational hardening. Remaining risk lives in:
- durability under crash/fault scenarios,
- clarity of experimental behavior (especially btrfs RW path),
- incomplete operational wiring (CLI mount path for managed backpressure/per-core hooks),
- benchmark/regression signal fidelity,
- unresolved spec open questions that can cause architecture drift if left ambiguous.

## Scope
This epic is fulfilled when all child epics below are complete:
1. Durable MVCC version-store implementation and replay hardening.
2. CLI mount wiring for managed backpressure/per-core scheduling.
3. btrfs experimental RW hardening and explicit compatibility contracts.
4. Fuzzing and adversarial test-depth expansion.
5. Benchmark fidelity and regression-guard upgrades.
6. Closure of formal open questions (with accepted decisions integrated into docs/tests/code).

## Non-goals
- Reopening excluded V1 scope items (multi-device btrfs, fscrypt, inline_data, etc.) unless explicitly approved in a separate scope-change epic.
- Marketing/docs polish without technical acceptance deltas.

## Definition of Done
- Child epics closed with evidence-backed acceptance criteria.
- FEATURE_PARITY.md, spec docs, and harness signals remain internally consistent.
- New work items include explicit test and observability requirements, not implementation-only checklists.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-03T21:48:35.532955522Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T22:07:04.030378904Z","closed_at":"2026-03-12T22:07:04.028905115Z","close_reason":"V1.1 Post-Parity Hardening and Operationalization COMPLETE. All 9 child epics closed (1: MVCC replay, 2: mount modes, 3: btrfs RW, 4: fuzzing, 5: benchmarks, 6: OQ decisions, 7: operator tooling, 8: program gate, 9: verification toolchain). 296 beads total, all closed. 3,594+ workspace tests pass. Program gate bd-h6nz.8: 13/13 PASS. RELEASE RECOMMENDATION: PROCEED.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["lane_program_root"],"comments":[{"id":294,"issue_id":"bd-h6nz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Execution sequencing recommendation (to avoid coordination deadlocks):
1) Start with `bd-h6nz.6.*` OQ closures that define policy boundaries.
2) In parallel, advance implementation-ready tracks that already have clear contracts (`bd-h6nz.2.*`, `bd-h6nz.4.*`, `bd-h6nz.5.*`).
3) Begin durable MVCC implementation (`bd-h6nz.1.*`) once OQ7/OQ4 decisions are available where required.
4) Use `bd-h6nz.7.*` after telemetry and behavior contracts are stable.

Reasoning:
- This order minimizes rework from policy churn.
- It keeps high-throughput execution by allowing independent tracks to proceed in parallel.
- It prevents communication purgatory by giving every agent a concrete lane with clear dependencies.","created_at":"2026-03-03T21:55:07Z"},{"id":347,"issue_id":"bd-h6nz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Plan-space optimization note (2026-03-03):\n- Removed over-broad cross-epic blockers (bd-h6nz.3<-bd-h6nz.6.3, bd-h6nz.5<-bd-h6nz.2, bd-h6nz.7<-{bd-h6nz.1,bd-h6nz.2,bd-h6nz.3}) to increase safe parallelism and avoid blocking noise.\n- Kept correctness constraints at task-level where they are actually needed (e.g., bd-h6nz.3.4 still depends on bd-h6nz.6.3).\n- Strengthened benchmark gate coupling with mount-mode gate (bd-h6nz.5.6 depends on bd-h6nz.2.6).\n- Added telemetry dependency for degraded-runtime benchmarking (bd-h6nz.5.4 depends on bd-h6nz.2.4).\nRationale: maximize throughput in plan space without reducing scope or lowering acceptance quality.","created_at":"2026-03-03T22:04:12Z"}]}

@@ -4626,7 +4626,11 @@ parser-side fast-commit replay surface in `ffs-journal` that decodes the FC tag
 stream, emits ordered logical operations only after a committed `TAIL`, and
 forces fallback when the FC stream is truncated or incomplete. Deterministic
 parser corpus coverage lives under `tests/fixtures/golden/ext4_fast_commit_*.json`
-and `crates/ffs-journal/tests/ext4_fast_commit_corpus.rs`.
+and `crates/ffs-journal/tests/ext4_fast_commit_corpus.rs`. `OpenFs` also
+collects mount-time fast-commit evidence from internal JBD2 journals that
+advertise `s_num_fc_blocks`, exposing the parsed tail stream via
+`ext4_fast_commit_replay()` with synthetic crash-image coverage for both clean
+replay and truncated fallback cases.
 
 The remaining gap is mount-time integration: extracting the FC region from the
 journal device and applying the parsed operations during ext4 open/recovery is

@@ -51,12 +51,14 @@
 | ext4 inline data read | `fs/ext4/inline.c` | ✅ | `read_ext4_inline_data()` reads from inode extent_bytes + system.data xattr. `INLINE_DATA` removed from rejected features. |
 | ext4 indirect block addressing | `fs/ext4/inode.c` | ✅ | `resolve_indirect_block()` handles direct + single/double/triple indirect pointers. `EXTENTS` no longer required at mount. |
 | ext4 FALLOC_FL_ZERO_RANGE | `fs/ext4/extents.c` | ✅ | ZERO_RANGE mode (0x10) zeroes allocated blocks in range via write path. |
-| ext4 fast commit replay | `fs/ext4/fast_commit.c` | ✅ | `replay_fast_commit()` in `ffs-journal` parses FC tag streams (HEAD/TAIL/INODE/ADD_RANGE/DEL_RANGE/CREAT/LINK/UNLINK/PAD), buffers operations until commit `TAIL`, and forces fallback when the stream is truncated or incomplete. Parser-side corpus coverage lives in `crates/ffs-journal/tests/ext4_fast_commit_corpus.rs`; mount-time extraction and application of the FC region are still tracked separately from this parser/evidence surface. |
+| ext4 fast commit replay | `fs/ext4/fast_commit.c` | ✅ | `replay_fast_commit()` in `ffs-journal` parses FC tag streams (HEAD/TAIL/INODE/ADD_RANGE/DEL_RANGE/CREAT/LINK/UNLINK/PAD), buffers operations until commit `TAIL`, and forces fallback when the stream is truncated or incomplete. `OpenFs` now also extracts deterministic fast-commit evidence from JBD2 journals that advertise `s_num_fc_blocks`, exposing mount-time FC tail parsing via `ext4_fast_commit_replay()` with synthetic image coverage for committed and truncated streams. FrankenFS still relies on full JBD2 replay for authoritative metadata recovery; FC operations are observed and reported, not yet applied to filesystem state during mount. |
 | btrfs tree-log replay | `fs/btrfs/tree-log.c` | ✅ | `replay_tree_log()` in ffs-btrfs walks tree-log tree when `log_root != 0`, returns items for FS tree merge. Wired into mount path. |
 | ext4 casefold (case-insensitive dirs) | `fs/ext4/namei.c` | ✅ | `lookup_in_dir_block_casefold()` with Unicode lowercase comparison. CASEFOLD removed from rejected features. |
 | ext4 fscrypt (nokey read-only mode) | `fs/ext4/crypto.c` | ✅ | ENCRYPT removed from rejected features. Encrypted filenames shown as raw bytes (nokey mode). Full decryption requires key management not in V1. |
 | btrfs multi-device RAID | `fs/btrfs/volumes.c` | ✅ | `BtrfsDeviceSet` in ffs-btrfs with multi-device read dispatch and stripe fallback. `read_logical()` resolves via `map_logical_to_stripes` with RAID0/1/5/6/10/DUP support. |
 | btrfs send/receive streams | `fs/btrfs/send.c` | ✅ | `parse_send_stream()` in ffs-btrfs parses the btrfs send stream format (magic, version, 22 command types, attribute TLV encoding). 3 parser tests. |
+| btrfs chunk tree walking | `fs/btrfs/volumes.c` | ✅ | `walk_chunk_tree()` in ffs-btrfs walks chunk tree beyond sys_chunk_array for complete chunk map. `parse_chunk_item()` parses individual chunk items. |
+| btrfs device tree discovery | `fs/btrfs/volumes.c` | ✅ | `walk_device_tree()` in ffs-btrfs walks device tree to enumerate all physical devices. |
 
 ### 2.1 btrfs Experimental RW Capability Contract (Machine-Checkable)

 `tests/fixtures/golden/ext4_fast_commit_*.json`. They are small, deterministic
 tag-stream oracles used by `ffs-journal` integration tests and do not rely on
-large `.img` artifacts.
+large `.img` artifacts. Mount-path coverage for the same feature also uses
+synthetic ext4 images built inline in `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs`, which lets
+the test suite exercise journal-superblock `s_num_fc_blocks` handling and
